question: [File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a transactionId whose getValidStartNs() returns a value near the maximum BigInt boundary so that maxConsensusTimestamp = BigInt(transactionId.getValidStartNs()) + maxTransactionConsensusTimestampRangeNs overflows or spans an astronomically wide timestamp range, forcing the DB to scan the entire transaction table and sustaining CPU/IO consumption above 30% compared to the preceding 24 hours?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transactionId with a valid_start_ns set to the earliest possible nanosecond timestamp (e.g., 0-0) so that the consensus_timestamp range [$2, $3] spans the entire historical transaction table, causing a full sequential scan on the transaction table and driving DB CPU usage above 30% of the preceding 24-hour baseline with a single request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ORDER BY (result = successTransactionResult) DESC, consensus_timestamp DESC clause in transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery by providing a payer_account_id that matches millions of rows in the timestamp range, forcing the DB to evaluate the boolean expression on every matching row before applying LIMIT 1, causing sustained CPU exhaustion above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user pass nonce=undefined (omitting the nonce parameter) so that the subquery SELECT coalesce($4, 0) is evaluated for every candidate row in the timestamp range, multiplying per-row subquery overhead and increasing DB CPU consumption by more than 30% compared to the preceding 24 hours?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly call the /contracts/results/:transactionIdOrHash endpoint with a valid but non-existent transactionId, causing getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId() to execute the full transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery range scan on every request without any result caching, sustaining DB read I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline through a moderate request rate that does not qualify as brute force?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() with a timestamp that matches a payer_account_id having a very large number of ethereum_transaction rows, causing the JOIN between ethereum_transaction and transaction tables to produce a large intermediate result set and driving DB memory and CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request that triggers getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() where the ethereumTransactionDetailsQuery fetches the CALL_DATA column (potentially a large blob) for every matched row, causing the DB to read and transmit large amounts of data per query and increasing network I/O and memory consumption on the mirror node above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that ethereumTransactionDetailsQuery performs an inner JOIN between ethereum_transaction (alias etht) and transaction (alias t) on both consensus_timestamp and payer_account_id without any LIMIT clause, so that a timestamp matching many ethereum transactions causes the DB to return an unbounded result set, exhausting DB memory and increasing node resource consumption above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() with a timestamp value that causes the encode(etht.value, 'hex') expression to be evaluated on a large binary VALUE column for every matched row, increasing DB CPU and I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline through a small number of carefully chosen requests?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetails()] Can an unprivileged external user cause getTransactionDetails() to instantiate a large array of Transaction objects by triggering a query that returns many rows, consuming significant Node.js heap memory and increasing process-level memory consumption above 30% of the 24-hour baseline without issuing a high volume of requests?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a transactionId with a payer_account_id that is a high-traffic account (e.g., a known exchange or contract account with millions of transactions) so that the WHERE payer_account_id = $1 AND consensus_timestamp BETWEEN $2 AND $3 clause matches a very large number of rows, causing the DB sort for ORDER BY to consume excessive memory and CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a LIMIT on the intermediate scan in transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery (LIMIT 1 only applies after ORDER BY) by choosing a payer_account_id and timestamp range that forces the DB to sort a large intermediate result set before returning a single row, causing a sort-spill to disk and increasing I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a transactionId where getValidStartNs() returns a string that, when converted via BigInt(), produces a value such that maxConsensusTimestamp = BigInt(validStartNs) + maxTransactionConsensusTimestampRangeNs spans the maximum configured range, and by choosing a payer_account_id with dense transaction history in that range, force a full index range scan that consumes DB CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline per request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the ethereumTransactionDetailsQuery JOIN path by calling /contracts/results/:transactionIdOrHash with a valid Ethereum transaction hash, causing getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() to execute a JOIN that reads ACCESS_LIST, AUTHORIZATION_LIST, CALL_DATA, and other large binary columns simultaneously, increasing DB I/O and memory above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() has no result size limit and maps all returned rows to new EthereumTransaction() objects, so that a timestamp matching many ethereum transactions causes unbounded JavaScript object allocation in the Node.js process, increasing heap memory consumption above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that maxTransactionConsensusTimestampRangeNs is a fixed global constant loaded from config at startup, and by choosing a valid_start_ns at the very beginning of the chain's history, ensure the timestamp range [$2, $3] covers the maximum possible window, causing the DB to scan the largest possible slice of the transaction table and increasing I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user send a moderate number of concurrent requests (below brute-force thresholds) each with a different valid payer_account_id but the same wide timestamp range, causing multiple simultaneous full-range scans on the transaction table that collectively increase DB CPU and I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() via the /contracts/results/:transactionIdOrHash endpoint with a transactionId that resolves to a consensus_timestamp where a large CALL_DATA blob is stored, causing the DB to read and serialize a multi-megabyte binary field and increasing network bandwidth and DB I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the nonce parameter path where nonce=0 is passed, causing the subquery SELECT coalesce($4, 0) to resolve to 0 and match all nonce=0 transactions in the timestamp range, forcing the DB to evaluate the ORDER BY boolean expression on a large set of rows and increasing CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery uses valid_start_ns = $2 as a filter but also scans consensus_timestamp >= $2 AND <= $3, so that on a high-traffic payer account the index on valid_start_ns may not be selective enough to prevent a large range scan on consensus_timestamp, causing DB CPU to exceed 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request that causes getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() to execute the ethereumTransactionDetailsQuery with a payerId matching a contract account that has submitted many ethereum transactions, causing the JOIN to produce a large result set that is fully materialized in DB memory before being returned, increasing DB memory consumption above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the BigInt arithmetic in maxConsensusTimestamp = BigInt(transactionId.getValidStartNs()) + maxTransactionConsensusTimestampRangeNs by providing a validStartNs string that, when parsed as BigInt, is very large but still valid, causing the computed maxConsensusTimestamp to wrap or produce an unexpectedly large range that forces a full table scan and increases DB I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetails()] Can an unprivileged external user cause getTransactionDetails() to be invoked with a query that returns a large number of Transaction rows, each of which triggers the Transaction constructor to parse multiple fields including nftTransfer array mapping and CustomFeeLimits instantiation, causing significant CPU overhead in the Node.js process and increasing server CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() where the ethereumTransactionDetailsQuery fetches the AUTHORIZATION_LIST column (a potentially large JSONB array for EIP-7702 transactions) for every matched row, causing the DB to deserialize and serialize large JSONB structures and increasing DB CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery has no query timeout enforced at the service layer, so that a carefully crafted payer_account_id and timestamp range that causes a slow DB query will hold a DB connection open for an extended period, reducing the available connection pool and increasing effective resource consumption above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?
